coredata.dc:title: Dynamic anti-counterfeiting and reversible multi-level encryption-decryption based on spirulina derived pH-responsive dual-emissive carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Lina
Wang, Yuli
Jia, Lei
Zhang, Xia
Xu, Jun
coredata.dc:description: Advanced encryption and anti-counterfeiting methods are urgently needed to protect valuable documents, merchandise and pharmaceuticals. In this research, dual-emissive carbon dots (d-CDs) featuring pH-responsive behavior are fabricated by using spirulina as precursor via a facile hydrothermal method. The d-CDs display two distinctive photoluminescence emission peaks at 456 and 677 nm under the excitation of 380 nm. Interestingly, the fluorescence color of d-CDs can be dynamically adjusted by modifying the pH of the system. Complex programmable red and blue luminous inks can be prepared from the d-CDs nanocomposite to protect the important data. Using triethylamine (TEA) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) as encryption and decryption reagents, the emission color of vital data can be switched repeatedly, demonstrating high potential of this safety inks for dynamic anti-counterfeiting, multiple data encryption-decryption as well as reversible data coding under UV light. Moreover, the intelligent two-channel sensor modified by chitosan (CN) CDs@CN realizes the reversible and synchronous conversion of luminescent color and sol-gel at different pH values. A logic gate system is final designed to dynamically monitor such interesting phenomenon. This work provides an efficient method for the construction of dual-emission CDs for dynamic anti-counterfeiting applications. Furthermore, the utilization of renewable and low-cost green biomass resources will have a positive effect on the sustainable development of CDs and their applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dynamic anti-counterfeiting
Encryption
Decryption
pH-responsive

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of luminescent inks for information security
Preparation of CDs@CN and sol-gel test
Optical properties and pH responsive behaviors of the d-CDs
Response mechanism of the d-CDs to pH
Dual channel response of pH by d-CDs@CN composites

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was partly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21805071 , No. 51773052 and No. U1904168 ), the Program for Science & Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (No. 20HASTIT015 ), the Science Fund for Excellent Young Scholars of Henan Province (No. 222300420047 ), Science and Technology Research Project of Henan Province (No. 222102320446 ), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Universities of Henan Province (No. NSFRF210331 ), the Innovative Research Team of Henan Polytechnic University ( T2021-2 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM of the d-CDs. (b) The size distribution of the d-CDs. (c) PXRD patterns of the d-CDs. (d) FTIR spectrum of the d-CDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of d-CDs, (b) C1s, (c) O1s and (d) N1s.

(a) Emission spectrum of the d-CDs at various pH conditions. (b) The corresponding CIE chromaticity coordinates graph of d-CDs. (c) Corresponding fluorescence photos under UV lamp at pH = 9.0–1.0. (d) pH reversibility test of the d-CDs.
(a) Logo information anti-counterfeiting process. (b) Document watermark anti-counterfeiting process. (c) Pattern anti-counterfeiting process.
(a) Encryption-decryption-encryption process of the intended protection information “CD” in the word “ENCODE”. (b) Conversion process of ASCII binary code of “CD”.
Schematic diagram of sol-gel and fluorescence dual-channel response under different pH conditions.
(a) Logic circuit based on sol-gel conversion. (b) Truth table of sol-gel conversion.
Schematic diagram of pH stimulus response of the d-CDs.

head.ce:title: Dynamic anti-counterfeiting and reversible multi-level encryption-decryption based on spirulina derived pH-responsive dual-emissive carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, No. 2001 Shiji Road Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, No. 2001 Shiji Road Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Henan Polytechnic University, #2001 Shiji Street, Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China.
Corresponding author. Henan Polytechnic University, #2001 Shiji Street, Jiaozuo, Henan, 454000, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Advanced encryption and anti-counterfeiting methods are urgently needed to protect valuable documents, merchandise and pharmaceuticals. In this research, dual-emissive carbon dots (d-CDs) featuring pH-responsive behavior are fabricated by using spirulina as precursor via a facile hydrothermal method. The d-CDs display two distinctive photoluminescence emission peaks at 456 and 677 nm under the excitation of 380 nm. Interestingly, the fluorescence color of d-CDs can be dynamically adjusted by modifying the pH of the system. Complex programmable red and blue luminous inks can be prepared from the d-CDs nanocomposite to protect the important data. Using triethylamine (TEA) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) as encryption and decryption reagents, the emission color of vital data can be switched repeatedly, demonstrating high potential of this safety inks for dynamic anti-counterfeiting, multiple data encryption-decryption as well as reversible data coding under UV light. Moreover, the intelligent two-channel sensor modified by chitosan (CN) CDs@CN realizes the reversible and synchronous conversion of luminescent color and sol-gel at different pH values. A logic gate system is final designed to dynamically monitor such interesting phenomenon. This work provides an efficient method for the construction of dual-emission CDs for dynamic anti-counterfeiting applications. Furthermore, the utilization of renewable and low-cost green biomass resources will have a positive effect on the sustainable development of CDs and their applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Accompanied by the rapid development of economy and modern science, information security (especially encryption and anti-counterfeiting) has attracted increasing attentions due to its significance in protecting valuable documents, merchandise, currency and pharmaceuticals [1]. During the past decades, miscellaneous security technologies involving radio-frequency identification [2], laser holography [3], nuclear track technology [4,5], and luminescence [6,7], have been used to store and read important information. In particular, luminescence holds the merits of facile design, low-cost and visibility, and thus is extensively used for encryption and anti-counterfeiting [8–10].

Lots of luminescent materials, such as semiconductor quantum dot [11,12], organic dye molecules [13], metal−organic frameworks [14–16], and lanthanide doped nanoparticles [17,18], have been developed and applied as luminescent inks for security designs [10]. However, their limitations such as high toxicity, photothermal instability, high-cost or low yield restrict their practical application [19]. Recently, perovskite nanocrystals [20,21], silica nanodots [22] and carbon dots [23] have also been applied in information security.

Notably, carbon dots (CDs) as novel luminescent materials hold versatile advantages such as high photostability, low-cost, low toxicity, good water solubility, facile fabrication process and good luminescence properties, and thus stand out in the fields of data storage and optical encryption. For instance, nitrogen and fluorine co-doped CDs with long-lived triplet excited states were synthesized by Long et al. which exhibited good performance in data storage [24]. Carbon dots embedded into PVA films were successfully fabricated by Qu et al. which demonstrated multilevel fluorescence data encryption [25]. Carbon dots with triple-mode emission were reported by Lin et al. which showed promise in the field of advanced anti-counterfeiting [8].

Nevertheless, most existing materials can only realize the transformation of a single fluorescent color under ambient light or ultraviolet light, and the state change is easy to be found, which is not conducive to the protection of confidential information [1,14,26–28]. However, stimuli-responsive smart fluorescent materials can perceive the surrounding stimuli (such as heat [29], force [30], light [8], acid and base [31], solvent vapor [32] etc.) and change the luminescence color under the external stimulation, which are difficult to mimic and duplicate, should be ideal for high-level confidential information protection [33]. Therefore, developing advanced alternative technologies that are simple to use, economical with excellent information storage capabilities has become a huge challenge in the field of confidential information storage and access.

Herein, a pH-responsive dual-emissive carbon dots (d-CDs) are fabricated by using spirulina as precursor via a facile hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). Spirulina is a photosynthetic cyanobacteria rich in essential amino acids and pigments such as chlorophyll a and xanthophylls [34,35], and has much lower cost than other renewable algae such as schizochytrium, chlorella and nannochloropsis. Notably, the use of renewable, low-cost green biomass as precursors to prepare CDs will surely promote the sustainable development of CDs and their applications. Two emission peaks located at 456 and 677 nm can be clearly observed in the photoluminescence spectrum of the d-CDs. Interestingly, the fluorescence color of the d-CDs can be dynamically regulated by adjusting the pH from acidic (red color) to alkaline (blue color). Red and blue fluorescent inks are further applied in optical dynamic anti-counterfeiting, encryption and encoding. Using triethylamine (TEA) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) as encryption and decryption reagents, the emission color of vital data can be switched repeatedly. Moreover, a smart dual-channel sensor device CDs@CN (CN = chitosan) realizes the reversible synchronous transitions of emission color and sol−gel when changing from acidic to alkaline. A logic gate system is designed to dynamically monitor such phenomenon. This material demonstrates high potential in multiple data encryption-decryption as well as reversible data coding under UV light.

This work provides valuable references for future design, synthesis and application of non-toxic, low-cost and smart responsive materials. To our knowledge, this is the first attempt to utilize pH responsive dual emissive carbon dots for reversible information encryption-decryption and erasable-rewritable data encoding field. Representative literatures on CDs-based anti-counterfeiting, encryption and data encoding in the past three years are listed in Table S1 [23,25,36–38], which exhibit interesting security application based on single-emission CDs with lifetime-encoded feature or room temperature phosphorescence property. Compared with these single-emission CDs mentioned above, the d-CDs in this study display unique dual-emission characteristic and impressive pH-responsive behavior, demonstrating potential for high security dynamic anti-counterfeiting and encryption applications.

Spirulina was acquired from Wudi Green Science Engineering Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China). Chitosan (CN) was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl), triethylamine (Et3N), sodium hydroxide, tris buffered saline and solvents with analytical grade were purchased from Energy Chemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was taken using a Tecnai-G2-F30 microscope. Infrared spectrum (IR) was performed by a Pristige IR21 FTIR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were collected on a PHI-550 versaprobe. The luminescence spectra were recorded by a Varian CARY Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer. UV–Vis absorption spectra were performed by a Varian UV-Cary 100 spectrophotometer. Elemental analysis was performed using an Elemonta Unicube elemental analyzer.

The d-CDs were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.6 g spirulina powder was dispersed in a mixed solvent (30 mL) of water and ethanol with a volume ratio of 2:3. After stirring at room temperature for 30 min, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel vessel and reacted at 150 °C for 6 h. Then the obtained mixture was cooled naturally and centrifuged to remove large particle residues. The obtained supernatant was passed through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and then dialyzed with dialysis membrane (MWCO 3500) for 24 h to remove the impurities. After lyophilization, dark-brown solid powers of d-CDs were obtained.

The d-CDs powers were dissolved into a series of Tris-HCl buffers solutions (pH = 1.0–9.0) and maintained the concentration of CDs was 0.5 mg/mL. The fluorescence emission spectra at different pH conditions were recorded at 380 nm excitation. To investigate the reversibility of the pH sensing process, the CDs powder was dissolved into 150 mL of Tris-HCl buffer to form a 0.5 mg/mL solution. Then HCl and NaOH solutions were added to the above CDs aqueous solution to adjust its pH repeatedly. These photoluminescence spectra at different pH values were recorded at 380 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of luminescent inks for information security
ce:section.ce:para: Two equal volumes (10 mL) of water-ethanol-glycerol solution (the ratio of the three is 1:3:1) were stirred for 1 h, and pH values were adjusted to 1.0 and 9.0, respectively. Finally, 30 mg CDs was dissolved in the above two solutions and stirred for 1 h to obtain the red and blue luminescence inks. In the process of information reading-hiding, after the filter paper with low background light containing information is fumigated with TEA steam, the information can be erased. In addition, important information can be encrypted by using commercial fluorescent ink and the ink prepared in this experiment for encoding and decoding experiments. Interestingly, the information can be decoded and recovered under the fumigation of TEA and HCl.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs@CN and sol-gel test
ce:section.ce:para: A total amount of 1 g chitosan (CN) and 1 g acetic acid were dissolved in 98 g purified water. Then the mixture was stirred and heated at 60 °C for 16 h to produce a stable and uniform chitosan solution (1 wt%). The detail of the sol-gel experiment is as follows: 4 mL aqueous solution of d-CDs (10 mg/mL) was added into 4 mL chitosan solution, and then the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 1 h to obtain a homogeneous solution. 1 mL of the above mixture was added into the colorimetric plate, followed by the addition of different volumes of TEA and HCl (3 M) to the colorimetric plate to observe the sol-gel conversion, as well as the color transition under 365 nm UV lamp.

The morphology and dispersion of the d-CDs were characterized by TEM characterization. As portrayed in Fig. 1 a, the d-CDs display high monodispersity and uniform distribution. The Gaussian fitting size distribution curves indicate a size distribution of 1.1–2.5 nm with mean diameter of 1.83 nm (Fig. 1b). As depicted in Fig. 1c, the XRD spectra of the d-CDs show a broad peak centered at 21.5°, revealing an amorphous carbon phase due to carbon atom disorder [39–41].

The FTIR and XPS spectra are collected to investigate the surface states and chemical composition of the d-CDs. In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1d) of d-CDs, the absorption band located around 3408 cm−1 may be ascribed to the stretching vibration of –OH [42,43], while that around 3282 cm−1 may be attributed to –NH stretching vibrations of porphin ring [44]. Two peaks located at 1651 and 1542 cm−1 may be originated from the stretching vibration of CO and CC, which indicate the existence of a multi-aromatic structure in CDs [45,46]. The characteristic peaks at 2925 and 1375 cm−1 may be caused by the stretching and bending vibrations of C–H and CH3 [42], while the peak at 1460 was attributed to the –CH2 scissoring vibration of pyrrole [44]. In addition, the absorption peak at 1080 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of C–O–C. For further verify the porphin structures, FTIR spectra of the tetraphenylporphyrin was collected (Fig. S1). The results indicated that the d-CDs and tetraphenylporphyrin have many common characteristics but a slight red-shift, manifesting that partial porphin rings still exist and form strong interactions with the d-CDs.

The XPS characterization further confirmed the detection results of FTIR. As portrayed in XPS survey spectrum of d-CDs (Fig. 2 a), typical peaks of C1s (285eV), N1s (400eV) and O1s (531eV) can be clearly observed, indicating the main component of the d-CDs. In addition, the C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) exhibits the existence of C–C/CC (284.7 eV), C–O (285.9 eV), CO (287.0 eV) and O–CO (287.9 eV) [43,45–47]. In O1s spectrum (Fig. 2c), four peaks can be observed which belong to CO (531.2 eV), O–H (532.0 eV), C–O (532.6 eV) and OC–C (533.5 eV) [43,45], respectively. In N1s spectrum (Fig. 2d), three peaks can be obviously observed, which are which are probably attributed to C–N (399.5 eV), N–H (399.8 eV) and pyrrolic N (400.3 eV) of porphin structures [43,44,48], respectively. Furthermore, the contents of O and N in the d-CDs are determined to be 35.23% and 10.75%, respectively, by using the elemental analysis. The above FTIR, XPS and elemental analysis characterizations can prove that the surfaces of d-CDs have porphin moieties and carboxyl groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and pH responsive behaviors of the d-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the d-CD are collected as depicted in Figs. S2 and S3. Four absorption bands around 224, 262, 405 and 667 nm can be observed from the absorbance spectrum (Fig. S2), in which the former two peaks are due to the π–π* transitions of sp2 domain of CC bonds, and the latter two peaks possibly stem from the n−π* transition due to the CN bonds from porphin structures and CO bonds [44,49,50]. As portrayed in Fig. S3, two emission maxima at 456 and 677 nm present in the PL spectrum of the d-CDs. An excitation-dependent properties can be observed in the blue emission region, while the red emission region shows independent feature at 677 nm (Fig. S4a). Notably, the peak at 677 nm correspond to typical emission from chlorophyll [44,51]. Similar red emission has previously been reported for CDs synthesized using biomass [52,53]. The optimal excitation wavelengths for the 456 and 677 nm emissions are 360 and 420 nm, respectively. The photoluminescence spectrum of the d-CDs is dominated by the emission of 456 nm under the excitation wavelength less than 380 nm, while that is dominated by the emission of 677 nm under the excitation wavelength larger than 400 nm, respectively. These results are consistent with the blue color emission of d-CDs under the 365 nm UV light and red color emission under the 420 nm lamp (Fig. S4b). Such excitation wavelength induced color modulability makes it potentially useful in dynamic anti-counterfeiting applications.

The absolute quantum yields of d-CDs was measured using an integrating sphere on a C9920-02G Quantum Efficiency Determination System (Hamamatsu Photonics, Japan). The d-CDs have a fluorescence QY of 3.72% under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm.

According to the most accepted PL mechanisms of CDs so far [44, 54–56], we infer that the luminescence mechanism of dual emission in d-CD is that the blue emission with excitation-dependent feature might originate from the carbon core state, while the red emission with excitation-independent behavior might stem from the surface porphin moiety. To exclude the existence of spirulina extract in d-CDs, the solution of d-CDs was subjected to dialysis. The small molecules were removed via a cellulose dialysis membrane (MWCO 3500) for 24 h. The UV–visible spectrum of unreacted spirulina extract is collected. As portrayed in Fig. S5, the untreated spirulina extract exhibits four typical bands of porphin at 450–700 nm. Compared with the extract, the d-CDs reduce the above four bands to one (Fig. S2), manifesting that the symmetry of the conjugated ring changes and a larger conjugated system is formed [44].

It is well known that porphyrin cannot be directly applied as fluorescent probe due to its hydrophobicity and instability. As depicted in Fig. S6, d-CDs showed much higher stability than untreated spirulina extract. In addition, good water solubility of the d-CDs also indicates the absence of hydrophobic fluorescent molecules from spirulina extract. To further verify our hypothesis on the luminescence mechanism of the d-CDs, the PL lifetime at 456 and 677 nm are monitored under the excitation wavelength of 365 nm (Fig. S7), which are 8.76 and 2.17 ns, respectively, indicating their different origins.

Furthermore, pH responsive behaviors of the d-CDs were also investigated. As depicted in Fig. 3 , the color of the d-CDs solution changes significantly from blue to red under the 365 nm UV lamp when the pH value changed from 9.0 to 1.0. Fig. 3a delineates the luminescence emission spectra of the d-CDs with different pH values. Under the excitation at 380 nm, the emission of 456 nm decreases gradually with the change of pH values from basic to acidic, which is almost completely quenched when the pH reaches to 1.0. Meanwhile, the emission peak at 677 nm also decreases gradually with the change of pH values from basic to acidic, and remains unchanged within the pH of 1.0–5.0. As demonstrated in Fig. 3b, the color coordinates of the CDs transform from blue to red region accompanied by the pH changes from 9.0 to 1.0, which is consistent with the trend of color changes of the CDs solution under UV lamp (Fig. 3c). The corresponding coordinate values are listed in Table S2. Moreover, the recyclability of the pH sensor was investigated by adjusting the pH value of the CDs repeatedly. As depicted in Fig. 3d, this pH sensor shows high reversibility after 5 cycles, exhibiting great prospect in pH response application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of the d-CDs to pH
ce:section.ce:para: It can be inferred from the above test results that the fluorescence color transformation phenomenon of the carbon dots at different pH may be caused by the aggregation or separation behavior of the material at different pH, as shown in Scheme 2 . The carboxyl groups on the surface of d-CDs may deprotonation and protonation at different pH values. The investigation of zeta potential of d-CDs at different pH values and the measurement of dispersion states of the materials at different pH values by TEM, validate the above assumption.

As shown in Fig. S8a, the obtained d-CDs show good monodispersity under alkaline conditions with mean size of 1.36 nm. Fig. S8b indicates that with the change of pH to acid (6.0), the particle size of d-CDs gradually increases and has a certain aggregation. Further aggregation happens when the pH is decreased to 1.0 (Fig. S8c), in which d-CDs self-assemble to larger particles with the regulation of the pH from alkaline to acidic conditions. Meanwhile, the abundant carboxy groups on the surface of d-CDs can generate a negatively charged surface. Thus, the zeta potential values of the d-CDs were then tested to confirm its surface states. Fig. S9 indicates the zeta potentials of the d-CDs at pH 9.0 and 5.0 are −11.2 and −4.10 mV, respectively. As the pH of the d-CDs is reduced to 1.0, the final zeta potential is −0.74 mV. These results are in consistent with negatively charged -COO- groups existing on the surface. In detail, by testing the zeta potential of d-CDs at different pH values, it can be proved that the carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs are deprotonated under alkaline conditions, and the dispersion can be improved by increasing the charge repulsion force caused by the negative surface charge. On the contrary, when the system is acidic, the dispersibility can be reduced by reducing the charge repulsive force and the fluorescence of the system is also quenched [43]. The results are in line with the TEM images and our assumption, which demonstrates that the as-synthesized d-CDs can be employed as potential pH probes.

The application of the above pH responsive dual-emissive CDs in multiple luminescent anti-counterfeiting are further investigated. Red luminescent ink made from the d-CDs is used to write letters “HPU” on the white filter paper, then red commercial ink is used to write "河南理工大学" below these letters (Fig. 4 a). Under the daylight, nothing can be observed on the filter paper. When the filter paper is placed under the 365 nm UV lamp, all data are clearly displayed in red, realizing the first stage of anti-counterfeiting. When the filter paper is exposed to the TEA vapor, the color of three letters of “HPU” changes from red to blue quickly, exhibiting the second stage and stimuli responsive dynamic anti-counterfeiting behavior. Compared to the single anti-counterfeiting technology of commercial ink, such carbon dots based dynamic anti-counterfeiting technology exhibit unique advantages, which are more difficult to imitate and duplicate. As shown in Fig. 4b, the d-CDs-based blue luminescence ink is used to write “HPU” on a document. Under the daylight, these letters are invisible, which can be observed as the anti-counterfeiting blue letters under the UV lamp (365 nm). Then, if the file is exposed to HCl vapor, the “HPU” converted to a red security flag under the UV light. This unique anti-counterfeiting technology can be used for the protection of some important documents, which has the advantages of secret and difficult to imitate.

In addition to text anti-counterfeiting, pattern is also one of the most important anti-counterfeiting methods. As delineated in Fig. 4c, the d-CDs based red and blue fluorescent inks are used to fill the lotus and leaves, respectively. Under the 365 nm UV lamp, red lotus and blue leaves can be observed. When the pattern is exposed to TEA vapor, the lotus convert to blue, and the process could be repeated.

Based on the polychromatic fluorescence color characteristics of the d-CDs with the change of pH, the application research of d-CDs in the field of information storage and reading was further investigated. As depicted in Fig. 5 a, the character “ENCODE” is written on a filter paper, in which the intended protection information “CD” is written using red d-CDs based luminescent ink, and the rest letters were written using the commercial red luminescent ink. Thus the intended protection information “CD” is encrypted in the word “ENCODE”. All writings are invisible under daylight, while red writing can be observed under a 365 nm UV lamp. Interestingly, the encrypted information “CD” can be decrypted with the TEA vapor due to the color transition of “CD” from red to blue, while the letter “ENOE” written by the commercial red luminescent ink maintains red color. More interestingly, the blue “CD” information can be erased by exposing in HCl vapor to prevent the leakage of information.

The ASCII binary encoding can be used to convert the letter “CD” into two ASCII codes, 01,000,011 and 01,000,100, respectively (Fig. 5b). The blue emission dots (d-CDs based luminescent ink) and red emission dots (commercial red luminescent ink) are defined as “1” and “0”, respectively, under the excitation of 365 nm. Firstly, the as-prepared d-CDs based- and commercial red luminescent inks are used to prepare the ASCII codes. Under ultraviolet light, fluorescent ink spots invisible under natural light appear red fluorescence and all the binary codes are translated into “00000000” and the information is encrypted in this stage. In the decryption stage, the right ASCII codes 01,000,011 and 01,000,100 can be readout when exposed to TEA vapor, due to the color conversion of letters “C” and “D” from red to blue with the TEA vapor. More interestingly, the information obtained by decoding can be erased again by HCl after being read. Therefore, important information can not only be encoded and decoded at any time through the above pH response process, but also be erased in time after the output of information. Compared with the reported encryption strategy, the obtained dual emission CDs are especially promising for applications in some high-level secret information protection systems using the multiple encrypted strategies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual channel response of pH by d-CDs@CN composites
ce:section.ce:para: Storing dynamic information in hydrogels is interesting because of their reprogramming and responsive properties. Herein, the sol−gel and emission synchronous conversion of d-CDs@CN nanocomposite were investigated. As shown in Fig. 6 , by adding TEA, the d-CDs@CN composite can be transformed from a solution state (with good fluidity) to an obvious gel state (with poor fluidity). Interestingly, the gel can return to a liquid solution again upon the addition of certain amount of HCl. More interestingly, the fluorescence color of the system changes repeatedly from red to blue along with the above phase transition. Continuous addition of NaOH can achieve the gelation of d-CDs@CN, while the introduction of HCl can adjust the pH of the system to acidity and break the gel state, indicating that the gel/sol transformation induced by pH is reversible. The above results confirm that the pH stimulation-responsive d-CDs@CN nanocomplex has the dual channel detection ability of fluorescence and sol-gel, which provides a broad application prospect for the rapid and sensitive detection of pH and regenerative gel for medical use.

In addition, a logic circuit for sol-gel conversion was also researched. As shown in Fig. 7 , TEA and HCl are used as input 1 and 2, respectively. The output represented the sol-gel state, in which the output “0″ and “1″ are defined to represent the sol and gel state, respectively. According to Fig. 7, when the input are (0 0), (0 1) and (1 1), the logic circuit outputs a “0″ signal, and only when the input are (1 0), the logic circuit output a “1″ signal, indicating the gel state under this input signal. Obviously, the gel state can only appear when TEA is present alone. In other words, the designed logic gate circuit has potential applications in pH sensitive stimulus detection.

In conclusion, a unique pH responsive dual emission carbon dots are fabricated and utilized for dynamic anti-counterfeiting, reversible information encryption-decryption and encoding of confident information. Moreover, a dual-channel memory composite d-CDs@CN is elaborately designed to realize the reversible cooperative transformation of emission color and sol-gel state. Due to the presence of CN in d-CDs@CN, the dual-channel memory system exhibits good pH reversible responsiveness, and the gel-sol transition occurs when the pH of the system changes from acid to alkaline, accompanied by the fluorescent color change from red to blue. Furthermore, a logic gate system is constructed to present the noteworthy variations of emission color and sol−gel upon the addition of acid or alkaline. This work provides a new strategy to utilize environmentally friendly and low-cost materials to realize high-level information encryption, intelligent detectors and circuit controllers, offering reference for the future exploration of advanced luminescent encryption and encoding materials.

Lina Zhang: Methodology, Data curation, Resources, Review and editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Yuli Wang: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writting original draft, Visualization. Xia Zhang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation. Lei Jia: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Review and editing, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Jun Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Review and editing, Visualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21805071, No. 51773052 and No. U1904168), the Program for Science & Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (No.20HASTIT015), the Science Fund for Excellent Young Scholars of Henan Province (No.222300420047), Science and Technology Research Project of Henan Province (No.222102320446), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Universities of Henan Province (No.NSFRF210331), the Innovative Research Team of Henan Polytechnic University (T2021-2).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2023.119727.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stimuli-responsive inks based on perovskite quantum dots for advanced full-color information encryption and decryption

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Sun, S. Su, Z. Gao, H. Liu, H. Wu, X. Shen, W. Bi, Stimuli-responsive inks based on perovskite quantum dots for advanced full-color information encryption and decryption, ACS Appl. Mater. Inter. 11 (2019) 8210-8216.

Jang, Wireless, room temperature volatile organic compound sensor based on polypyrrole nanoparticle immobilized ultrahigh frequency radio frequency identification tag, ACS Appl. Mater. Inter. 8 (2016) 33139-33147.
